35 min read
Just now

--

TL;DR
Long story short, for some applications, C++ is the ultimate solution, but in other cases, FPGAs and GPUs may be the way forward. This article focuses on migration from C++ to FPGAs and how FPGAs can outperform both CPU and GPU by orders of magnitude when designed properly.
To give a rough idea on what this article is about, I will demonstrate how a 70$ FPGA beats a 3.5GHz high-performance CPU cranking a telemetry packet processing applcation written in C++ by a factor of 25x (yes, you read that right!), all while only 1.1% of the FPGA being in use!!! And that’s just the begining…
Unfortunately, I can’t really fit much more into a TL;DR section of an article this complex, and I do invite you to read the rest.

1. Introduction
When we talk about computers, the picture that comes to peoples mind is almost always instruction-set based processors (a.k.a. CPUs). To the average person (and even to many software developers) the CPU is the computer. They produce assembly instructions (either directly or through some high-level language like C/C++) and the CPU executes them.
But have you ever wondered why it is done this way? After all, the CPU itself is made of transistors, and we could have just laid the transistors in a way that achieved the same goal, probably much faster and less power-hungry?
To understand why, we have to look back at the early days of computer engineering, long before Integrated Development Environments (IDEs) or automated chip layout tools existed. In the mid-20th century, designing a specialized physical circuit (also known as ASICs) for every single unique application was mission-impossible. There were no software tools to compile code into hardware, and more importantly, unlike today, transistors were an incredibly scarce, expensive luxury.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 1. Intel 4004 Chip (property of Intel)This extreme limitation on the number of available transistors dictated the architecture of early computing. One simply couldn’t afford to build dedicated hardware structures for separate tasks. Instead, the economic and physical reality favored a brilliant compromise: build a single, compact execution unit (the CPU) and feed it instructions one at a time. By using a clock to sequence these instructions (with frequency denoted in Hertz or Hz), a small, fixed set of hardware gates could be reused millions of times over to perform completely different tasks.
As this instruction-driven architecture took hold, software languages evolved alongside it to make programming manageable (COBOL, C, C++, Pascal, etc.). In the beginning, engineers wrote raw Assembly, which was a direct, 1:1 mapping of human-readable text to the CPU’s physical instruction set (I did that too back then for embedded solutions like 8051 or AVR).
While powerful, writing complex applications in Assembly was agonizingly slow and error-prone. It offered the most extreme level of performance, with the lowest level of productivity and maintainability. After all, assembly was nowhere near human language, so no surprise there.
To bridge this gap, languages like C emerged in the 1970s, later superseded by C++ for more complex designs. The ultimate goal of these high-level languages was to allow developers to express logic in a language closer to human thought and mathematics. It was up to the compiler to translate the written code sequential instructions the CPU needed.
For over half a century, this symbiotic relationship between the CPU instruction set and sequential software languages shaped the way the world wrote code. But the world changed. Moore’s Law advanced, manufacturing exploded, and the transistor deficit I mentioned earlier vanished into history. Suddenly, we had billions of transistors at our disposal on a single die, along with the sophisticated EDA software tools required to configure them. This shift paved the way for Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs).
Unless you work directly in the hardware domain, there is a high chance you have never interacted with an FPGA. They exist in the shadows of data centers, high-frequency trading racks, and advanced defense systems, and frankly, they’re super fun to work with.
For high-performance applications, however, they make the ultimate difference. While a CPU forces your C++ loops to wait in line through a narrow sequential instruction pipeline, an FPGA pushes data through their pipelines with no additional latency, and in parallel.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 2. CPU vs. FPGA Architecture2. What are FPGAs?
In a Nutshell, FPGAs emulate direct transistor-to-transistor implementation. I said emulate, because it can’t really go in and fuse transistors together based on your design (that would take anywhere between a 60k$ to 20M$+).
2. 1. The Internals of FPGAs
Instead, FPGAs are comprised of building blocks called look-up-tables (or LUTs for short). A LUT is a configurable digital block with a number of inputs and one or two outputs. You can program this LUT to perform various operations on the input to produce the output:

• It can add or subtract inputs

• It can perform AND, OR, XOR, NOT, NAND, etc. (basic gate operations)

• It can turn into a small distributed RAM cell

• It can turn into a multiplexer, decoder, encoder, shift-register, etc.
Note that LUTs are usually coupled with one or two single-bit memory cells known as Flip-Flops (D-FlipFLop to be exact). For example the Xilinx (now AMD) Spartan 6 cells contained two flip-flops per LUTs (the chip architecture was quite register-rich, where Altera (now Intel) Cyclone IV had only one flip-flop per LUTs. On the flip-side however, Cyclone IV had much more routing resources, and fitting your design in the chip was a rather enjoyable experience.
Once you successfully finished coding your design, it will be up to the toolchain to see how it could creatively implement it using LUTs of different configuration, memory cells, IOs, etc..
In addition to LUTs, the FPGA fabric contains the following building blocks:

• Switching Matrix: These are your routing resources, allowing you to connect LUTs together to form a larger and more complex solution. For some reason most FPGA datasheets omit this critical characteristic, as it’s pretty much a make-or-break when choosing FPGAs. An FPGA poor in routing resources cannot be filled beyond like 50% or 60% without massive loss of performance (or even complete Place-and-Route failure).

• Memory Blocks: Known as BlockRAMs, these often dual-port cells contain large chunks of memory (for example 64KB). Their main objective is to provide memory resources so you don’t end up using all of the precious LUTs in the FPGA to fulfill your memory needs.

• Digital Signal Processing (DSP) blocks: Also known as MACs, these blocks perform multiply-and-accumulate operations (hence the name ‘MAC’). Although this comes very cheap in CPUs, FPGAs operate at bit level, so these will consume too many LUTs if you were to not use DSP blocks.

• Hard-Core IP blocks: These could be high-speed memory-controllers, high-speed transceiver IOs for PCIe, USB-C or any other high-speed differential signalling.

• Clock Generators (PLLs and DLLs): These building blocks provide high-speed high-strength clock signal for your chip. PLLs can reach multi-GHz speeds, and DLLs (being simpler) can be used to produe phase-shifted clocks and clocks at 2x frequency.

• I/O Blocks: These blocks allow your design to connect to an actual pin on the device, connecting you to the outside world (they could support various signalling standards, DDR, etc.)
See content credentials
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 3. FPGA Fabrique (known as Floor-plan)Code written for FPGA does not produce sequential-logic line-by-line executables. You are essentially describing how these building blocks are to be configured and connected to each other to implement the functionality you are engineering. Hardware Description Languages (HDL) such as Verilog or VHDL dominate the space and that is what you will likely spend most of your time (there are also High-Level Synthesis approaches, I’ll get to those late).
When you’re done with coding, it’s time to “compile” your design. Here is what happens when you do so in an FPGA environment:

• The synthesizer (in FPGAs we ‘synthesize’ our design we do, not ‘compile’) from the tool-chain will break your design down into these building blocks (RAM, IO, LUT, DSP, etc.). The product of this stage is called the “RTL” (i.e. ‘Register Transfer Level’).

• The Static-Timing-Analysis (STA) is performed post-synthesis. Here the analyzer knows what components are involved and the kind of performance it could expect from them. But it has no idea where those components are located on the board yet, so it performs a preliminary assessment based on “Estimated Wire-Load Model”.

• The P&R (place and route) engine will place your design into LUTs and cells based on the target frequency of your design and various timing constraints (these are super important, I’ll get to them later). The process is guided actively by STA, and the netlist gets updated iteratively.

• Post-Route STA is performed once more when the design is fully routed. Now the analyzer can calculate exact delay between components, setup-times, hold-times, etc. A report will be produced to reflect the exact performance characteristics of the design, and top critical pathways that failed to meet timing requirements (if any), which could then be used to further refine the design.

• The Bitstream Generator converts your routed design into a “Bitstream” once your design is successfully synthesized and routed. You then have to send the content of this file bit-by-bit to the FPGA everytime on startup. FPGAs are RAM-based, and lose their firmware (or in FPGA lingo ‘gateware’) once power is cut off.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 4. FPGA Synthesis, Implementation & Bitstream Generation FlowUnlike CPU-based programs, your FPGA design almost never carries any dead weight: Any building block in your design that is left unused will be optimized away. The synthesizer is quite good at this, and will discard any building block that cannot be electronically connected to the rest of the system. In fact, it has no other choice but to do, as it simply can’t let unconnected logic float around on the chip somewhere.
One of the biggest culture shocks software engineers face when moving to FPGAs is the nature of synthesis warnings. In the CPU world, a compiler warning usually hints at a minor optimization opportunity, a readability improvement, or a potential edge-case bug. Your program still compiles, runs, and behaves deterministically.
When designing for FPGAs, a single warning can quietly and literally dismantle your entire architecture. Hardware synthesis engines are aggressively optimized, and they prune away any logic that they deem “dead” or unreachable. If you forget to connect a single critical wire in your top-level design (such as a clock signal, a reset line, or an output port) the synthesizer won’t throw a compilation error. Instead, it silently concludes that the disconnected module is completely useless.
Without a single error or build failure, the tool will happily optimize away 80% of your design, leaving behind an empty silicon shell. The build completes successfully, but you will spend the next two weeks staring at an oscilloscope or logic analyzer wondering why your hardware is completely dead.
In hardware design, an error is a blessing because it stops the build. A warning is a ghost that you have to actively hunt through thousands of lines of log files to find.

3. CPU vs. FPGA Execution Environment
To fully understand how FPGAs work, we need to first understand how CPUs work. I assume you are familiar to some extent with CPUs, but for the sake of completeness, I’ll include this section anyway.
